nativeRange.habitat: Heavily vegetated lowland peat swamps and slow-moving blackwater streams with extremely soft, acidic, tannin-stained water; substrate typically dark organic matter and leaf litter

decorPreferences: floating plants (Salvinia, Riccia) for surface cover and bubble-nest anchorage
driftwood roots and branches to create dim shaded areas
leaf litter (Indian almond/catappa leaves) to release tannins and lower pH
Microsorum, Taxiphyllum, or Cryptocoryne as midground planting
clay pots or PVC piping as shelters and spawning caves

diet.notes: Primarily insectivorous in the wild, feeding on odonate (dragonfly and damselfly) nymphs and other small invertebrates. In captivity, offer live or frozen Daphnia, Artemia (brine shrimp), bloodworm (Chironomus larvae), glassworms, and black mosquito larvae as the dietary backbone. Will usually accept high-quality micro-pellets and small dried foods once acclimated, but live and frozen items should form the majority of the diet to maintain optimal colour and condition. Avoid overfeeding — obesity is a documented risk.

goodWith: small peaceful cyprinids (e.g., Boraras species) in larger tanks
nano rasboras of similar size and water preference
avoidWith: other Betta species (hybridisation and fighting risk)
fast or boisterous fish that cause stress

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species image for Betta simorum; verify current availability before uploading to R2.

summary: Sim’s Betta is a slender wild Betta from the blackwater peat swamps of Sumatra’s Jambi and Riau provinces. Males display vivid iridescent green scale markings on a dark brown body. Demanding water-chemistry requirements (near-zero hardness, pH 4–6.5) place it firmly in intermediate-to-specialist territory; not suited to standard community setups.
careNotes:
Betta simorum belongs to the B. bellica species group and was described by Tan & Ng in 1996, named for Thomas G. K. Sim and his wife Farrah of Sindo Aquarium Pte. Ltd. in Singapore. Wild specimens originate from heavily vegetated lowland peat swamps in Jambi Province and adjacent Riau Province, Sumatra, where the water is extremely soft (GH and KH near zero), very acidic (pH as low as 3–4 in nature), dark with dissolved tannins and humic acids, and slow-moving. Replicating these blackwater conditions is the principal challenge: most tap water must be treated or replaced with RO water re-mineralised only very lightly, then conditioned with dried catappa (Indian almond) leaves, alder cones, or peat filtration to lower pH and add tannins. Target pH 4.0–6.5, GH 0–5 dGH, and KH as close to zero as practical.

A tank of at least 60 cm in length (approximately 60 L) is sufficient for a single pair. Filtration should be gentle — an air-powered sponge filter turned over slowly is ideal; strong flow stresses this species. Keep lighting subdued; under bright light fish often appear washed out and show longitudinal stress bars. Floating plants (Salvinia, Riccia) and surface cover are important both to dim the light and to provide attachment points for the bubble nest. Include driftwood, leaf litter, and emergent planting (Microsorum, Cryptocoryne) to break sightlines and provide refuge for females after spawning. The tank must have a tight-fitting lid: Betta simorum is a confirmed jumper, and the labyrinth organ requires access to warm, humid air directly above the water surface — do not fill the tank completely to the top.

Conspecific aggression between males is moderate to high; the species is best kept as single pairs or singly. Mixed-species community tanks are possible with very peaceful, similarly-sized blackwater fish (small Boraras, Sundadanio) but vigorous or much larger tankmates will stress or intimidate this species. Water changes of around 10–15% weekly using pre-conditioned soft acidic water help maintain stability without pH shock. IUCN lists B. simorum as Vulnerable (2019) due to ongoing peat-swamp habitat loss in Sumatra.
breedingNotes: Betta simorum is a paternal bubblenester. The male constructs a loose bubble nest at the water surface, often beneath floating plants or against the edge of a leaf. After vigorous courtship embraces, the female releases eggs which the male collects and deposits into the nest. The male guards and tends the nest aggressively, and the female should be removed or provided with dense cover once spawning is complete to avoid post-spawn attacks. Eggs hatch in approximately 24–48 hours at typical tank temperatures; fry become free-swimming a further 3–4 days later. First foods should be infusorians or commercially prepared fry foods, transitioning to microworms and freshly hatched Artemia nauplii as the fry grow. The male continues to guard fry and should be removed once they are fully free-swimming and feeding independently. Breeding is best attempted with a dedicated species-only tank with very soft, acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0, GH ≤2), slightly elevated temperature (27–29°C), and minimal water movement.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Betta simorum
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided native range (Jambi and Riau, Sumatra; Kapuas River system, West Kalimantan), habitat description (blackwater peat swamps), water parameters (pH 3.0–6.0, hardness 18–90 ppm, temperature 25–30°C), adult SL 80–90 mm, tank minimum 80×30 cm base, diet (odonate nymphs; Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm in captivity), behavior (best kept alone or with very peaceful species), and care notes (dim lighting, sponge filtration, tight-fitting lid, catappa leaves). Note: SeriouslyFish describes B. simorum as a bubblenester in the bellica group; mouthbrooder flag is set per task brief constraint — this conflicts with SeriouslyFish and IBC Species Maintenance Program data and should be reviewed.

additional.name: FishBase — Betta simorum
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 6.4 cm SL, taxonomy (Osphronemidae, Anabantiformes, subfamily Macropodusinae), native range (Indonesia), facultative air-breathing, trophic level 3.2 (carnivore/omnivore), IUCN Vulnerable (2019), human uses: aquarium trade and commercial fisheries.

additional.name: IBC Species Maintenance Program — Betta simorum
additional.notes: Confirmed bubblenester; water parameters pH 6–7, GH 0–1, KH 0–1; range includes Jambi, Rantau Panjang, Sumatra and Bangka Island; maximum 12 cm TL reported (outlier); pairs kept in 20 gal minimum with caves and plants.

additional.name: Aquarium Glaser GmbH — Betta simorum
additional.notes: Confirmed soft acidic blackwater requirements; noted species does best at 26–28°C; nitrate should not exceed 100 mg/L; diet: live/frozen Daphnia, Artemia, bloodworm; occasional dried food accepted.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Betta simorum
additional.notes: Confirmed adult size 6.4 cm SL; taxonomy (Osphronemidae); described by Tan & Ng 1996; named for Thomas G. K. Sim and wife Farrah of Sindo Aquarium Pte. Ltd.; endemic to Indonesia (Jambi, Sumatra); IUCN Vulnerable (2019).

fish.breedingNotes: Paternal bubblenester; male builds nest under floating plants and guards clutch. Eggs hatch in 24–48 h; fry free-swimming after a further 3–4 days. Requires dedicated species tank with very soft acidic water (pH 5.0–6.0, GH ≤2) and minimal flow. Female must be separated or well-covered after spawning. First foods: infusorians then microworms and Artemia nauplii.
